Thoroughly survey the build step scripts in Tools/rbw/rbgjb/ and how they're referenced. I need:

1. **Full inventory**: List every script in Tools/rbw/rbgjb/ with its filename and a one-line description of what it does (read the first comment block or function name).

2. **Pipeline membership**: Which scripts belong to the single-arch pipeline vs the multi-platform pipeline vs shared between both?

3. **Stitch function references**: Find the stitch function(s) that define step_defs arrays referencing these scripts. Search for "step_defs", "rbgjb", "stitch" in Tools/rbw/ and related files. Show the exact step_defs array contents.

4. **Other references**: Search the entire repo for any other references to rbgjb filenames — docs, memos, CLAUDE.md, paddock files, tabtargets, etc. Show file and line for each reference.

5. **File-touch bitmap references**: Check the orient/show output files and the JJ gallops data for references to these filenames.

Be very thorough — enumerate every reference so we know what needs updating during renames.

160→
   161→**Approach**: Switch from docker buildx to Google's kaniko builder for multi-platform images
   162→
   163→**Pros**:
   164→- Kaniko designed for containerized builds without docker daemon
   165→- Native GCP integration, handles authentication differently
   166→- Official Google solution for Cloud Build multi-platform
   167→
   168→**Cons**:
   169→- Complete rewrite of build steps (rbgjb06-rbgjb09)
   170→- Different Dockerfile syntax requirements
   171→- Less familiar tooling
   172→
   173→**Status**: Architectural pivot, pursue if auth solutions fail
   174→
   175→=== Option 4: Single-Architecture Build (amd64 only)
   176→
   177→**Approach**: Abandon multi-platform requirement, build only `linux/amd64` using default docker driver
   178→
   179→**Pros**:
   180→- Works immediately with existing authentication
   181→- No complexity, stable solution
   182→- Sufficient for most current deployments
   183→
   184→**Cons**:
   185→- No ARM support (excludes Raspberry Pi, ARM cloud instances, Apple Silicon native)
   186→- Limits future flexibility
   187→- Defeats original multi-platform goal
   188→
   189→**Status**: Fallback if all other options prove infeasible
   190→
   191→=== Option 5: Manual Multi-Architecture via Manifest Combining
   192→
   193→**Approach**: Build each architecture separately using default driver, manually combine into manifest list
   194→
   195→**Pros**:
   196→- Uses working authentication (default driver)
   197→- Achieves multi-platform result
   198→- No BuildKit isolation issues
   199→
   200→**Cons**:
   201→- Complex build orchestration (3 separate builds)
   202→- Manual manifest creation and push
   203→- Slower (sequential builds instead of parallel)
   204→- Significant implementation effort
   205→
   206→**Status**: Complex workaround, consider only if simpler options fail
   207→
   208→=== Option 6: OCI Layout Bridge (Implemented Solution)
   209→
   210→**Approach**: Separate build phase from push phase using OCI Image Layout as intermediate format
   211→
   212→**Key insight**: Instead of fighting BuildKit's isolation to make `--push` work, break the atomic operation into discrete phases with filesystem storage as bridge.
   213→
   214→**Implementation** (proven working 2025-12-31, build 3b544930):
   215→----
   216→# Step 6: Build to OCI tarball (no network push)
   217→docker buildx create --driver docker-container --name rb-builder --use
   218→docker buildx build \
   219→  --platform="${_RBGY_PLATFORMS}" \
   220→  --output type=oci,dest=/workspace/oci-layout.tar \
   221→  --tag ${_RBGY_MONIKER}:${TAG_BASE}-img \
   222→  --label "moniker=${_RBGY_MONIKER}" \
   223→  --label "git.commit=${_RBGY_GIT_COMMIT}" \
   224→  -f "${_RBGY_DOCKERFILE}" \
   225→  .
   226→
   227→# Step 7: Push OCI archive with crane (has authentication)
   228→tar xf /workspace/oci-layout.tar -C /workspace/oci-layout
   229→
   230→crane push /workspace/oci-layout \
   231→  "${_RBGY_GAR_LOCATION}-docker.pkg.dev/${_RBGY_GAR_PROJECT}/${_RBGY_GAR_REPOSITORY}/${_RBGY_MONIKER}:${TAG_BASE}-img" \
   232→  --index
   233→----
   234→
   235→NOTE: crane uses Docker credential helper configured by `gcloud auth configure-docker` (GCB step 03). The OCI tarball must be extracted before pushing; crane reads OCI layout directories with `--index` flag for multi-platform images.
   236→
   237→**Pros**:
   238→- BuildKit never needs registry credentials - only writes local filesystem
   239→- crane runs as Cloud Build step with Docker credential helper access (native GCP auth)
   240→- Build phase can take hours, push phase takes seconds (no credential timeout)
   241→- Preserves all multi-platform capabilities
   242→- Uses Google's own tools (crane from go-containerregistry project)
   243→- Separates concerns: BuildKit does what it's good at (building), crane does what it's good at (registry operations)
   244→- `/workspace` is shared across Cloud Build steps (perfect for intermediate storage)
   245→
   246→**Cons**:
   247→- Requires crane/gcrane container image (`gcr.io/go-containerregistry/gcrane`)
   248→- Two-step process instead of atomic build-and-push
   249→- Need to adjust SBOM generation step (Syft can read OCI layouts after extraction)
   250→
   251→**Why this works**:
   252→- Docker buildx OCI export: Official Docker feature, documented for multi-platform
   253→- crane OCI→Docker: Official capability in Google's go-containerregistry project
   254→- Cloud Build + crane: Google's own tool designed for container registry operations
   255→- Addresses root cause: Separates build (no auth needed) from push (has auth)
   256→
   257→**References**:
   258→- https://docs.docker.com/build/exporters/oci-docker/[Docker OCI exporter documentation]
   259→- https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/five-ways-skopeo-can-simplify-your-google-cloud-container-workflow[Google Cloud Blog: Five ways Skopeo can simplify your Google Cloud container workflow]
   260→- https://manpages.debian.org/unstable/skopeo/skopeo-copy.1.en.html[Skopeo manpage: OCI transport]
   261→- https://eng.d2iq.com/blog/a-tale-of-two-container-image-tools-skopeo-and-crane/[Skopeo vs Crane comparison]
   262→
   263→**Status**: Recommended solution, ready for implementation
   264→

   281→

   285→  --password-stdin "https://us-central1-docker.pkg.dev"

   287→----
   288→
   289→This authentication pattern **works for the host docker daemon** but doesn't propagate to isolated buildx containers.
   290→
   291→**Step 4: QEMU for Cross-Platform** (rbgjb04-qemu-binfmt.sh)

content.content:
1→==== Decision
     2→
     3→Cloud Build configuration files for trigger-based builds use `cloudbuild.json` format,
     4→generated programmatically via `jq`.
     5→YAML is not used.
     6→
     7→==== Rationale
     8→
     9→JSON is the Cloud Build REST API's native wire format.
    10→The `projects.builds.create` and `projects.triggers.run` endpoints accept JSON request bodies
    11→modeled on the API's `Build` resource.
    12→YAML is a file-authoring convenience that gets parsed into the same `Build` structure before submission.
    13→
    14→Programmatic generation via `jq` produces correct JSON directly — no YAML indentation
    15→fragility, no multiline string quoting hazards, no dependency on `yq` or custom emitters.
    16→The stitch function (`zrbf_stitch_build_json`) already generates JSON;
    17→converting to YAML would add complexity with zero functional benefit.
    18→
    19→==== Evidence
    20→
    21→[cols="1,3",options="header"]
    22→|===
    23→|Claim
    24→|Source
    25→
    26→|REST API is natively JSON
    27→|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.builds/create[projects.builds.create API reference] — request body is a `Build` resource in JSON
    28→
    29→|YAML and JSON are equivalent serializations
    30→|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/build-config-file-schema[Build configuration file schema] — "You can write the build config file using the YAML or the JSON syntax"
    31→
    32→|Triggers accept both formats
    33→|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/automating-builds/create-manage-triggers[Create and manage build triggers] — "Cloud Build configuration file (yaml or json)"
    34→
    35→|Trigger autodetect precedence includes JSON
    36→|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/api/reference/rest/v1/projects.triggers[projects.triggers REST resource] — precedence: `cloudbuild.yaml`, `cloudbuild.yml`, `cloudbuild.json`, `Dockerfile`
    37→
    38→|No deprecation signals through Feb 2026
    39→|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/release-notes[Cloud Build release notes] — no mention of reducing JSON support
    40→
    41→|SLSA provenance does not differ by format
    42→|https://docs.google.com/build/docs/securing-builds/generate-validate-build-provenance[Generate and validate build provenance] — provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` (file name), not format-specific fields
    43→|===
    44→
    45→==== What Dies
    46→
    47→* Any assumption that `cloudbuild.yaml` is required for triggers or SLSA provenance
    48→* The linked term `rbtgr_build_yaml` — replaced by
    49→{rbtgr_build_json}
    50→* YAML-specific concerns: multiline string quoting, indentation sensitivity, `$$` escaping conventions
    51→
    52→==== What Survives
    53→
    54→* `zrbf_stitch_build_json()` — continues to emit JSON via `jq`, adapted for trigger structure
    55→* `rbgjb/*.sh` step scripts — inlined into JSON `args` arrays at inscribe time
    56→* All `_RBGY_*` substitution variables — baked into JSON `substitutions` object
    57→* OCI Layout Bridge pattern — unchanged
    58→
    59→==== Provenance Note
    60→
    61→SLSA v1.0 provenance records `buildConfigSource.path` which will contain `cloudbuild.json`.
    62→This is a cosmetic difference from `cloudbuild.yaml` — the structured provenance fields
    63→(build inputs, parameters, outputs) are identical regardless of config file format.
    64→
    65→==== References
    66→
    67→* Research conducted: 2026-02-24 (web search + API documentation review)
    68→* Google docs consistently present YAML examples by convention, not by requirement
    69→* The `gcloud builds submit` CLI internally converts any config format to JSON for API submission
    70→
